fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

This is an application by the litigation guardian for the applicant to make an order under s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) that the provisions of s 102NA(2) of the Act apply in these proceedings. The effect of that would mean that the parties to the proceedings would not be able to personally cross-examine each other. Reading between the lines, the application is, more to the point, intended to provide an avenue whereby the litigation guardian for the applicant might secure some form of Legal Aid funding so that legal representatives can be engaged. She said as much to me on the last appearance.
Between [3] and [9] and then again at [18] in Owen & Owen [2020] FamCA 90; [2020] 60 Fam LR 334, Gill J pointed out that there are two preconditions for the engagement of s 102NA(2) irrespective of whether the engagement comes about by force of s 102NA(1)(c)(i) to (iii) or by an order of a Court made pursuant to s 102NA(1)(c)(iv). Here, it is not entirely clear that those two preconditions are satisfied. Whilst it might be the case that, in these proceedings, the applicant and the respondent will need to be cross-examined, indeed that is a probability I think, it is not necessarily the case that cross-examination is a certainty. Given the nature of these proceedings – an application for the declaration of nullity – it is not at all a fait accompli, as it were, that there will be cross-examination. The second precondition, it seems is fulfilled. There is an allegation of family violence between these parties, although the allegation of family violence is ill-particularised.
The making of an order pursuant to s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) is discretionary. Thus, in a case where s 102NA(2) is engaged because of one of the other provisions of s 102NA(1)(c), there will be no occasion for the exercise of the discretion. The other subsections of s 102NA(1(c)), that is ss (c)(i), (ii) and (iii), are not engaged here on the evidence before me, and so the only way in which s 102NA(2) might be engaged is through the making of a discretionary order.
Subsection 102NA(1)(c)(iv) and the provisions of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) more generally provide no guidance for the exercise of the discretion provided by that subsection. The discretion is unfettered but, no doubt, must be exercised judicially. That usually requires the identification of the factors relevant to the exercise of the discretion in each particular case and a weighing of those factors to arrive at a conclusion. There is nothing in the text of the relevant provisions that suggests that a favourable exercise of the discretion is available just for the asking. To take such an approach would be to subvert the clear legislative intention that absent satisfaction of one or other of ss 102NA(1)(c)(i) to (iii), there is no automatic prohibition upon cross-examination.
The purpose of the legislation conferring the discretion will always be relevant. So much was discussed by Hogan J in Hurley & Melton (No 2) [2020] FamCA 917; [2020] 61 Fam LR 405. In that case, her Honour considered the context in which the amendments to the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) that inserted s 102NA came to be made and the explanatory memorandum of the Bill for their introduction. Similar considerations were undertaken by Gill J in Owen, the case to which I have already referred.
Here, the evidence before me consists of an affidavit sworn by the litigation guardian for the applicant. It is very brief, being only four paragraphs long. The operative part of the affidavit says that:
I can confirm that the applicant has been subject to domestic abuse by the respondent wife.
It goes on to assert that the applicant has suffered physical and emotional harm between January and September 2005 and although what is described as an ADVO, which I take to mean an apprehended domestic violence order, was applied for, none was ever made. It seems that there were some undertakings given but the giving of an undertaking is not the same as the making of an order. That is the extent of the evidence. It is woefully insufficient to secure a favourable exercise of the discretion. There is nothing in the evidence before me that would suggest that the making of the order is necessary.
Accordingly, I dismiss the application in a proceeding.
I need to say some other things. In her written submissions filed in support of this application, the litigation guardian asserts that she is permitted to act on behalf of and appear for the applicant during the trial because a judicial registrar made an order on 19 November 2025 permitting that course. She interprets that order as giving her a right of appearance for the applicant at any hearings. The problem with that argument is that the judicial registrar made no such order. The order that was made on 19 November 2025 and amended on 22 December 2025 was this:
Pursuant to rule 3.15 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021, [Ms B Bongiovani] is appointed as the litigation guardian for the applicant, [Mr Bongiovani].
Otherwise, the application in a proceeding that was before the judicial registrar was dismissed. All that order does is make the appointment of the litigation guardian. It says nothing about permission to act or appear for the applicant at a trial.
The proposition that the litigation guardian here might properly represent the applicant by appearing for him personally in court is at odds with the usual practice in relation to litigation guardians. Litigation guardians who are not lawyers ordinarily must instruct solicitors. That has been the position at common law for a very long time and although it is not the subject of a particular rule in this Court, it is the subject of rules in other Courts. For example, the Queensland Supreme Court has a rule, rule 93(3) in the Uniform Civil Procedure Rules 1999, which provides that a party's litigation guardian who is not a solicitor may act only by a solicitor. There is a similar rule in the rules of the High Court of Australia.
The absence of such a rule in this Court, in my view, does not change the usual position. There are some very good reasons why a litigation guardian who is not a lawyer should not represent the relevant party in court. The present application is a good example of one of those reasons. A litigation guardian's obligation is to conduct legal proceedings on behalf of the person whom they represent in accordance with the best interests of the party they are representing.
If a litigation guardian is not a legal practitioner, then it cannot be said that they are conducting the proceedings in the best interests of the person they represent if they have not obtained legal advice. That is even more so when it comes to representation in court. It could only be the case that a party's interests are properly represented by a lawyer in court. A litigation guardian who is not a lawyer does not fulfil that role and does not discharge their duty as a litigation guardian in those circumstances. As I say, the present application is a good example of the operation of the rule. The application with which I have just dealt was inadequately prepared and hopeless.
The evidence used to support it was entirely inadequate but, yet, proper legal advice and proper legal representation might have seen a different outcome, although I suspect not, but one never knows. The applicant in the proceedings, the person for whom Ms B Bongiovani acts as litigation guardian, has been denied the opportunity of having a successful outcome on that application because of her incompetence.
What I intend to do is to list this application for a show cause hearing in about a month's time calling upon Ms B Bongiovani to show cause why either (a) she should not be removed as a litigation guardian in the case, given her clear incompetence, or (b) why the proceeding should not be stayed until such time as the litigation guardian has instructed a lawyer. I will set down that show cause hearing for hearing and determination before me on 13 April at 9.30 am.
